summary: India is where I was born and where my family comes from. Although I left India before I could even walk, it remains the place where the language and traditions that ground me first entered my life. When I moved back to India after living abroad, I expected to feel at home, but I felt distant because I had changed and so had the way I saw things.

Bhutan was my first home after India, where I moved just six months after I was born. It taught me stillness and peace, with its misty mountains and quiet roads. I remember the comfort of knowing few people but knowing them well, and it set the tone for a life that never stood still.

England was the first place where I attended school, and everything felt different there—structured, formal, familiar but foreign. I had to wear a uniform, queue politely, and adjust to gray skies and different spellings of familiar words. It was an important early experience of adapting to a new culture and education system.

Jakarta, Indonesia, was bursting with energy and life when I lived there. The streets were alive with scooters, food carts, and people, and I played barefoot soccer in alleys. It was loud, friendly, and full of movement, teaching me how warm and open a city could be.

Indonesia, specifically Jakarta, was a place full of energy and warmth during my childhood. Living there was a sharp contrast to other places I lived, with its loud, friendly streets and open community. It was a formative experience that showed me a different way of life, full of movement and connection.

Frankfurt, Germany, was a place of order and precision that I moved to after the chaos of Jakarta. It was one of the hardest adjustments I made because I had to slow down and fit into a calm, punctual environment. The city’s structured and neat atmosphere taught me to read silences and be deliberate in conversations.

Germany, particularly Frankfurt, was a significant chapter in my life where I learned to slow down and adapt to a more structured culture. Moving from Indonesia’s lively streets to Germany’s calm and orderly environment was a big shift. It challenged me to fit in without losing myself and to appreciate deliberate, quiet interactions.

Helsinki, Finland, gave me quiet and space to think during my time there. It taught me that silence doesn’t mean disinterest and that deep relationships can grow slowly and steadily, like a winter thaw. This experience helped me appreciate a different pace of life and the value of patience in building connections.

Living in Finland, especially in Helsinki, was a time of quiet and introspection for me. The culture showed me that silence can be meaningful and that relationships develop gradually. It was a place that contrasted with my earlier experiences and helped me grow in understanding patience and depth in connections.

In the Czech Republic, I experienced a growing independence as I wandered the streets of Prague alone. The city, rich with stories and history, invited curiosity and allowed me to be myself without needing to explain. It was a place where I felt comfortable exploring my identity in a new way.

Prague, in the Czech Republic, was a city where I found a sense of independence and curiosity. Walking its streets alone, I discovered a place steeped in history that welcomed me to be myself without explanation. It was a meaningful chapter in my journey of identity and belonging.

Now, I am in the United States pursuing my education. The U.S. values individuality but constantly asks me to define myself—where I’m from, what my story is, who I am. Living here as an international student, I continue to navigate questions of identity and belonging in a place that prizes self-definition.

My story is part of the Immigrant Stories project at the University of Minnesota, where personal narratives about migration and identity are preserved and shared. This initiative by the Immigration History Research Center has helped me reflect on my journey across eight countries. Being connected to the University of Minnesota has given my story a platform to reach others.

The Immigration History Research Center at the University of Minnesota runs the Immigrant Stories project, which includes my story. Their goal to preserve and share personal migration narratives has allowed me to contribute my experiences of moving across eight countries. This center has been instrumental in helping me and others explore identity and belonging through storytelling.
